content.text: For
multi-step agent workflows
, the best progress UI is not a generic spinner but a
stateful step model

that shows what the agent is doing now, what already finished, what is waiting, and where human input may be required. That fits Claude especially well because both tool use and Managed Agents are explicitly event-driven systems: Claude can emit tool-use requests, your app or Anthropic executes work, results come back as events, and long-running Managed Agent sessions persist event history that you can surface in the UI.

attrs.alt: Diagram: Progress indicators for multi-step agent workflows

content.text: Core principle
A good progress indicator for agent workflows should answer four user questions at all times:
What is happening, what just happened, what is next, and do I need to do anything?

If the UI cannot answer those four questions, users experience the agent as a black box, even when the backend is functioning correctly. For Claude-powered workflows, transparency matters even more because tool use and autonomous execution can span multiple phases and pauses.
Why simple spinners fail
A spinner works for short, single-step waits, but multi-step agents are not single-step waits. Claude’s tool-use loop involves structured
tool_use
and
tool_result

exchanges, and Managed Agents sessions can run for minutes or hours with persistent state, streamed responses, and mid-execution steering or interruption. So a single “loading” indicator hides the very information users need to build trust: step decomposition, active phase, progress boundaries, and pause states.
Best mental model
Treat the workflow as a
timeline of discrete states
, not a blob of model output. A useful high-level state machine is:
queued
planning
tool-selection
tool-running
waiting-on-result
summarizing
awaiting-human
completed
failed
interrupted
This maps well to Anthropic’s documented tool-use and Managed Agents event concepts, where sessions exchange user turns, tool results, and status updates over time.
What progress should show
A strong agent workflow progress UI should usually include these elements:
Current phase.
Completed phases.
Pending phases.
Active tool or subtask.
Elapsed time.
Whether the workflow is blocked on human approval.
Whether work is parallel or sequential.
Final output or artifacts when ready.
This is more useful than a numerical percentage alone, because many agent workflows are not linear enough for percentage to mean much.
Claude-specific grounding
Anthropic’s tool-use docs explain that client tools run in your application when Claude returns
stop_reason: "tool_use"
and one or more
tool_use
blocks, after which your application executes the operation and returns a
tool_result

. Anthropic’s Managed Agents overview defines sessions and events explicitly, saying sessions are stateful, long-running, and stream results through SSE while persisting event history server-side. These two facts imply a very important frontend rule:
progress indicators should be event-driven, not guess-driven
Pattern 1: step checklist

The most universal pattern is a checklist or stepper where completed steps get a checkmark, the active step pulses, and upcoming steps remain muted. This works especially well when the workflow is mostly sequential, such as “Understand request -> Search -> Analyze -> Draft answer.” It is simple, readable, and trustworthy because users can immediately locate the current step relative to the whole workflow.
Pattern 2: live event timeline

For more complex agent runs, a timestamped event timeline is often better than a rigid linear stepper. Browserbase’s Managed Agents documentation in the results emphasizes surfacing session events and status updates back to your own UI, while commentary around Managed Agents highlights a live event log of tool calls, results, and status changes. This pattern is valuable when the agent branches, retries, or calls different tools dynamically, because the timeline can show reality without forcing it into an overly neat sequence.
Pattern 3: plan-and-execute
A high-trust pattern is to show an initial
plan

and then transition into execution. Fuselab’s design guidance in the results calls out “plan-and-execute” as one of the patterns that consistently survives deployment and user testing for enterprise agent interfaces. In practice, this means the agent first surfaces a compact plan such as “1. Gather sources, 2. Compare findings, 3. Draft recommendation,” then each plan item becomes a tracked progress row as work proceeds.
Pattern 4: tool disclosure rows

Tool use should be visible when it affects waiting time or trust. Since Anthropic explicitly describes server tools and client tools as structured capabilities, the UI can expose “Searching web,” “Fetching URL,” or “Running code” as named tool rows rather than as mysterious pauses. This makes the workflow legible without exposing raw low-level protocol noise.
Pattern 5: approval gates
A very important progress state is
awaiting human action
. Managed Agents documentation states that you can steer or interrupt an agent mid-execution by sending additional user events, and the managed-agents review result describes
requires_action

states where the agent pauses for human approval or rejection before continuing. In the UI, this should not look like “stalled” or “loading”; it should appear as a distinct approval gate with clear options like Approve, Reject, or Revise.
Pattern 6: artifacts alongside progress

When the agent produces files, code, charts, or intermediate outputs, show them next to the progress stream rather than burying them at the end. The Managed Agents review in the results describes a split view with a live event log and an artifacts panel, which is a very strong pattern for making long runs feel productive before the final summary arrives. This is especially useful for Claude workflows that generate meaningful intermediate work products rather than only final text.
Sequential vs parallel work

Progress indicators should distinguish between sequential and parallel tasks. Claude Code multi-agent guidance in the results mentions spawned or background agents with their own progress indicators, while TodoWrite orchestration guidance describes updating multiple parallel tasks as each completes. In the frontend, parallel work is best shown either as grouped subtasks under one phase or as small concurrent task chips with independent statuses, not as one fake linear bar.
Exactly one active step
For sequential workflows, the UI should usually show
exactly one in-progress step
at a time. The TodoWrite orchestration guidance in the results explicitly recommends exactly one
in_progress

task except in parallel execution, which is a useful UI discipline too. This prevents ambiguous displays where multiple steps look active even though the user cannot tell which one is truly driving progress.
Good status labels
Good labels are action-oriented and user-comprehensible. Examples include:
“Understanding your request”
“Searching internal documentation”
“Comparing results”
“Generating final summary”
“Waiting for your approval”
These are better than low-level internal labels like
phase_2_exec
or vague terms like
processing
because they explain the work in human terms.
Time indicators

Elapsed time is often more honest than percentage complete. Claude Code multi-agent guidance in the results mentions showing elapsed time in progress displays, and status/activity guidance recommends time estimates and activity histories as part of real-time awareness. For agent workflows with unknown branching, “Running for 2m 18s” is often clearer than “62% complete,” unless your system truly has a bounded known step count.
Percentage bars: use carefully

A percentage bar is appropriate only when the workflow actually has measurable bounded progress, such as 3 of 5 subtasks completed or 7 of 10 files processed. For open-ended reasoning or dynamic tool selection, percentage can be misleading because Claude may decide to add steps as it learns more. In those cases, step count or phase labels are safer than fabricated completion percentages.
Event normalization layer

The frontend should not render raw provider events directly. Instead, build a normalization layer on the server or in the client state model that converts Claude tool-use or Managed Agent events into app-level statuses such as
phase_started
step_completed
awaiting_approval
artifact_created
, or
workflow_failed
. This is important because Anthropic’s raw event shapes are protocol-level, while good progress indicators need product-level semantics.
Recommended data model
A solid progress model often looks like this:
This gives the UI enough structure to support steppers, timelines, parallel groups, and approval gates without hardcoding every workflow.
React / Next.js rendering pattern
A practical React layout is often a three-part view:
Left: conversation or user instructions.
Center: workflow progress board or event timeline.
Right: artifacts, tool results, or approval cards.

The shadcn AI agent workflow block in the results demonstrates a stage-column pattern with step cards, live status, duration badges, and connected stages, which is a good fit when you want a more operational dashboard feel. For simpler chat products, a collapsible inline progress card inside the assistant message is often enough.
Streaming vs polling implications

Realtime streaming is ideal, but progress patterns should also degrade gracefully to polling. The Vercel Labs Managed Agents starter referenced in the results uses polling rather than true client-side streaming, which means users see batched updates rather than token-by-token progress. That implies your UI should be able to handle both granular live events and coarser periodic snapshots without changing the mental model.
Failure states

A strong progress UI treats failure as a first-class state. If a tool call fails or the agent is interrupted, mark the affected step as failed or cancelled, preserve completed work, and show a recovery action such as Retry, Continue from here, or Ask for help. Do not reset the whole workflow to blank, because that destroys trust and hides useful partial work.
Multi-agent workflows

If you have multiple cooperating agents, show them as nested tracks or grouped workstreams, not as one flattened list. Claude Code guidance in the results notes that spawned or background agents can appear with their own progress indicators and cancellation controls, which suggests a UI model where the parent workflow contains child agents with their own elapsed times and statuses. This is clearer than forcing all work into one monolithic sequence.
Good UX rule
The strongest rule is:
make invisible work visible, but compress low-value noise

. Users usually want to see phases, key tool actions, blockers, and outputs, but not every token or every microscopic internal event. So the right progress indicator is selective transparency, not raw log dumping.
Practical recommendation
For most Claude-based multi-step workflows, start with a
step checklist + live event timeline + approval state + artifact panel

. That combination covers the main needs: where the workflow is, what the agent is doing, when the human must intervene, and what useful outputs already exist. It also maps naturally to Anthropic’s event-driven tool-use and Managed Agents models.
Interview-ready explanation

A strong concise explanation is: “For multi-step Claude agent workflows, I avoid generic spinners and instead build event-driven progress UI. I normalize Claude tool-use or Managed Agents events into product-level workflow states, render them as a stepper or timeline with exactly one active sequential step unless tasks are parallel, expose tool-use and approval gates explicitly, and surface intermediate artifacts alongside progress. The goal is to show what’s happening now, what finished, what’s blocked, and whether the user needs to act.”
Best Practices and Implementation Guidelines

When working with Progress indicators for multi-step agent workflows, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance. The guidelines below reflect real-world lessons learned from production deployments and extensive developer experience.
Start with the simplest implementation that meets your requirements, then optimize based on measured performance data rather than assumptions.
Document your decisions and trade-offs explicitly — future maintainers (including yourself) will benefit from understanding why certain choices were made.
Implement comprehensive error handling from the beginning rather than retrofitting it later; error paths are often the most complex code paths.
Use structured logging throughout your implementation to make debugging and monitoring significantly easier in production environments.
Write tests for both the happy path and edge cases, paying special attention to boundary conditions and error scenarios.
Review and audit your implementation regularly as requirements evolve — what was optimal yesterday may not be optimal today.
Leverage existing libraries and frameworks when appropriate rather than rebuilding well-solved problems from scratch.
Monitor production behavior continuously and establish alerting thresholds based on real usage patterns.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding common pitfalls helps you avoid costly mistakes. The following issues appear frequently in implementations and can be prevented with proper awareness and planning.
Mistake 1: Insufficient Error Handling

Many implementations handle the success path thoroughly but neglect error scenarios. Every external call can fail, and every assumption can be violated. Build error handling as a first-class concern, not an afterthought.
Mistake 2: Ignoring Rate Limits and Quotas
Production systems frequently hit rate limits when traffic spikes occur. Implement exponential backoff with jitter from the start to handle these gracefully.
Mistake 3: Not Validating Inputs and Outputs
Trusting data at boundaries leads to subtle bugs that are hard to diagnose. Always validate inputs before processing and outputs before returning them to callers.
Production Considerations

Moving from development to production introduces challenges that do not appear in local testing. The following considerations apply to virtually all production deployments and should be addressed before launching.
Latency under load: Performance characteristics change significantly when multiple users make concurrent requests. Load test your implementation before launch.
Memory management: Long-running processes accumulate state. Implement proper cleanup and monitor memory consumption over time.
Configuration management: Production secrets, API keys, and environment-specific settings must be managed securely and separately from application code.
Graceful degradation: When dependencies fail, your system should degrade gracefully rather than failing completely. Define fallback behaviors for each dependency.
Observability: You cannot fix what you cannot see. Implement metrics, tracing, and logging before you need to diagnose production issues.
Deployment strategy: Blue-green deployments or canary releases allow you to validate changes with a subset of traffic before full rollout.
Rollback plan: Every deployment should have a documented rollback procedure that can be executed quickly if issues arise.
Security review: Production code is a target. Review for injection vulnerabilities, authentication issues, and data exposure risks.
Performance Optimization Strategies

Performance optimization should be data-driven. Measure first, then optimize the actual bottlenecks rather than guessing. The following strategies apply broadly but should be applied selectively based on profiling data.
Strategy
When to Apply
Expected Benefit
Trade-offs
Caching
Repeated identical requests
Significant latency reduction
Stale data risk, memory usage
Connection pooling
High-concurrency scenarios
Reduced connection overhead
Resource management complexity
Async processing
I/O-bound workloads
Better throughput
Complexity, ordering challenges
Batching requests
Multiple independent operations
Fewer round trips
Increased latency per item
Pagination
Large result sets
Reduced memory and latency
Multiple requests needed
Compression
Large payloads over network
Reduced bandwidth costs
CPU overhead for compression
Testing Strategy

A comprehensive testing strategy ensures your implementation behaves correctly across all scenarios and continues to work as requirements evolve. Structure your tests in layers, from fast unit tests to slower integration tests.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock

class TestImplementation:
    def test_happy_path(self, client):
        """Test the primary success scenario."""
        result = client.process(valid_input)
        assert result.status == 'success'
        assert result.data is not None

    def test_error_handling(self, client):
        """Test that errors are handled gracefully."""
        with pytest.raises(ValueError, match='Expected error message'):
            client.process(invalid_input)

    @patch('module.external_dependency')
    def test_with_mocked_dependency(self, mock_dep, client):
        """Test behavior when external dependency is unavailable."""
        mock_dep.side_effect = ConnectionError('Service unavailable')
        result = client.process(valid_input)
        assert result.status == 'degraded'
        assert result.fallback_used is True

    def test_boundary_conditions(self, client):
        """Test edge cases at the boundaries of valid input."""
        assert client.process(empty_input).status == 'no_op'
        assert client.process(max_size_input).status == 'success'
        with pytest.raises(ValueError):
            client.process(over_limit_input)
Key Takeaways

The concepts covered in this sub-subtopic are foundational to building reliable, production-ready implementations. Revisit these principles when making architectural decisions and when debugging issues that arise in production.
Correctness first, performance second — optimize only after establishing that the implementation is correct.
Error handling is not optional — every failure mode should have a defined behavior.
Observability is an investment that pays dividends during incidents and outages.
Testing at multiple levels (unit, integration, end-to-end) provides confidence and catches different categories of bugs.
Document your decisions and trade-offs to help future maintainers understand the system.
Production systems face challenges that development environments hide — plan for them explicitly.
Iterate on your implementation based on real-world feedback and measured performance data.
Apply these principles consistently across your implementations to build systems that are maintainable, reliable, and performant in production environments.
Best Practices
When working with progress indicators for multi-step agent workflows, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with progress indicators for multi-step agent workflows. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
Performance and Scalability
As your usage of progress indicators for multi-step agent workflows scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
